japanese: 天ぷら海老うどんセット

food_subgroup: Japanese noodle set meal with tempura seafood and sushi

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tempura prawn udon set meal. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers udon noodles as a category. No specific code exists for a tempura prawn udon set meal bundle; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for tempura prawn udon set meal. SNOMED CT does not have granular Japanese noodle dish codes at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 set meal (udon bowl + 1 sushi piece + 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving comprising approximately 300 g of tempura prawn udon (broth, noodles, and one tempura prawn), one piece of sushi (approximately 30–40 g), and one beverage (approximately 200–250 ml), totalling an estimated 550 g edible weight excluding beverage volume.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodle soup with tempura prawn — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for udon noodles (NDB 20110), shrimp tempura (NDB 15152 adapted with frying adjustment), dashi broth, and sushi rice components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2nd edition) for regional validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a tempura prawn udon set meal bundle. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: udon noodles (cooked, ~150 g), tempura prawn (2 pieces, ~60 g), dashi-soy broth (~120 ml), and sushi rice with one sushi piece (~50 g). USDA SR Legacy values used for each component. Sushi component adds approximately 5–8 g carbohydrate and minor protein. Beverage component (tea or soft drink) excluded from per-100 g solid-food calculation. Values represent the solid edible portion of the udon bowl plus sushi, normalised to 100 g. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; udon noodles GI reference (GI ~55, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles (cooked), which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate component of this dish. Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283 reports udon noodles GI ~55 (glucose scale). The sushi rice component has a higher GI (~72) but constitutes a minor fraction of the total carbohydrate in this bundle; the blended GI is estimated at approximately 55–58. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 38) / 100 ≈ 20.9, where carbohydrate per serving ≈ 38 g (based on 380 g solid edible portion at 13.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, excluding beverage).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi-soy broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1,600 mg per serving from the udon broth alone), which may significantly contribute to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming only half the broth is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from udon noodles and sushi rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that this set meal carries a moderate glycemic load (estimated GL ~21 per serving) driven primarily by the udon noodles and sushi rice; portion control, choosing a smaller noodle serving, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables or protein can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate dietary cholesterol from tempura prawn
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the elevated sodium from the broth and moderate cholesterol contribution from the tempura prawn (approximately 45 mg cholesterol per 100 g); limiting broth intake and choosing steamed or grilled protein alternatives when available is recommended to support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tempura Prawn Udon Bundle (天ぷら海老うどんセット) is a Japanese set meal offered at Sushi Express in Singapore, comprising a bowl of udon noodle soup topped with one or two pieces of tempura prawn (ebi), one piece of customer-selected sushi, and a beverage. Udon noodles are thick, chewy wheat-flour noodles served in a savoury dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce and mirin, giving the soup a light umami flavour with mild sweetness. The tempura prawn is coated in a light, airy wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp, providing a contrasting texture to the soft noodles. The sushi component varies by customer choice and may include nigiri or maki rolls. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal providing approximately 98 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient from the noodles and rice, moderate protein from the prawn, and relatively low fat. Sodium content is notably high due to the dashi-soy broth, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low) based on the udon noodle component, with a glycemic load of approximately 21 per serving, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains wheat gluten, crustaceans, soy, and fish-derived dashi, making it unsuitable for those with allergies to these ingredients. It is not vegetarian, vegan, or certified halal. This set meal is a popular affordable Japanese dining option in Singapore's fast-casual restaurant segment.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tempura prawn udon?
Is tempura udon high in sodium?
What is in a Sushi Express bundle?
What allergens are in tempura udon?

ingredient_synonyms.tempura: light batter deep-fried seafood or vegetables
ingredient_synonyms.dashi: Japanese soup stock made from kombu and bonito flakes
ingredient_synonyms.mirin: sweet Japanese rice wine used for seasoning
ingredient_synonyms.nori: dried seaweed sheet used in sushi

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific set meal bundle. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for udon noodles, tempura prawn, dashi broth, and sushi rice. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for udon noodles as the dominant glycemic component. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach and the variable sushi and beverage components.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates normalised to 100 g of the solid edible portion (udon bowl plus sushi piece), excluding the beverage component, which is variable and may be tea, soft drink, or water. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this composite estimate. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients under standard preparation conditions. 6. The sushi component is variable (customer choice); the nutrient estimate assumes one standard nigiri piece (~30 g) with a moderate-GI topping. 7. The beverage component is excluded from per-100 g calculations. 8. Sodium content is notably high and primarily attributable to the dashi-soy broth; individuals on sodium-restricted diets should consume only a portion of the broth. 9. GI estimated at 55 from University of Sydney GI Database udon noodle reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). 10. FoodOn match is closest_parent (noodle dish); no exact FoodOn term exists for tempura prawn udon set meal. 11. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (udon noodles) applied as closest_parent; no bundle-level FoodEx2 code exists.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Constructed full RESULT JSON from scratch using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimation for nutrient values.
Iteration 1: Applied University of Sydney GI Database udon noodle reference (GI 55) as analogous estimate with full documentation.
Iteration 1: Added Japanese local name 天ぷら海老うどんセット to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Japanese cuisine.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes field to satisfy G3.
Iteration 1: Confirmed trans_fat_g 0.0 is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data; documented in notes to satisfy G4 zero-macro justification rule.
Iteration 1: Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Iteration 1: Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only (closest_parent, none); no invented codes used.
Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index 55, resolving the q4_glycemic_completeness failure.
